## earnings/01_historical-financials.md

# Historical Financials — TMCV (Tata Motors Limited, formerly TML Commercial Vehicles Limited)

**Reporting regime:** India / SEBI-LODR. Reporting standard: Ind AS. Currency: INR (₹ crores). Fiscal year ends March 31. TMCV was incorporated June 23, 2024 and received the demerged CV business effective October 1, 2025 (appointed date July 1, 2025). FY26 (April 1, 2025 to March 31, 2026) is the first full 12-month fiscal year for this entity on a consolidated basis. The FY25 comparative in the audited filings covers only nine months (June 23, 2024 to March 31, 2025) and is explicitly flagged as not directly comparable.

**Critical data limitation:** No audited multi-year annual history exists for this entity beyond FY26. The FY25 figure is a nine-month period (not annualised in the filing). Pre-demerger years (FY23, FY24) are available only as CV-segment carve-out data from the old Tata Motors consolidated group — they are not audited stand-alone financials for TMCV. The annual table below covers FY23–FY26 using CV-segment data for FY23–FY24 (source: MD&A commentary and transcript) and audited consolidated data for FY25 (nine months) and FY26 (full year). FY23 and FY24 figures are marked as carve-out/unaudited segment data. YoY growth rates where periods are not directly comparable are noted accordingly.

---

## 1. Annual Financial Table

*Currency: INR ₹ crores. FY = April 1 to March 31. FY23 and FY24 are CV-segment carve-out estimates from transcript and MD&A commentary (not audited stand-alone); FY25 is audited consolidated for nine months only (June 23, 2024 to March 31, 2025); FY26 is audited consolidated for the full twelve months (April 1, 2025 to March 31, 2026). Reported numbers used throughout; where the company also discloses an "Underlying" metric (EBITDA, EBIT), both are shown. EBITDA defined as: Underlying EBITDA = operating profit before product development expenses charged to P&L, realised FX and commodity hedges, fair-value loss on FVTPL investments, MTM on FX/commodity hedges, and exceptional items. Reported EBITDA computed as: Revenue from operations − material costs − employee costs − other expenses + depreciation (i.e., EBIT before dep/amort and before exceptional items and finance costs). FCF = company-defined: CFO less capex (net of asset sale proceeds), plus interest received, plus dividends from equity-accounted investees, less investments in equity-accounted investees of core auto entities.*

| Metric | FY23 (CV segment est.) | FY24 (CV segment est.) | FY25 (9-month period) | FY26 (full year) | Trend |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|
| Revenue from operations (₹ cr) | ~66,000 [1] | ~71,000 [1] | 58,217 [2] | 83,855 [3] | Inflecting |
| Revenue YoY % | — | ~+7.6% [1] | N/A (9M) | +9.8% vs annualised FY25 [3a] | Inflecting |
| Gross Profit (₹ cr) [note A] | N/A | N/A | 19,021 [4] | 27,123 [4] | Stable |
| Gross Margin % | N/A | N/A | 32.7% | 32.3% | Stable |
| Underlying EBITDA (₹ cr) [note B] | ~5,100 [1] | ~7,200 [1] | 6,595 [2a] | 10,314 [3] | Accelerating |
| Underlying EBITDA Margin % | ~7.8% [1] | ~10.1% [1] | 11.3% [2a] | 12.3% [3] | Accelerating |
| Underlying EBIT (₹ cr) [note B] | ~3,600 [1] | ~5,600 [1] | 5,030 [2a] | 8,538 [3] | Accelerating |
| Underlying EBIT Margin % | ~5.5% [1] | ~7.9% [1] | 8.6% [2a] | 10.2% [3] | Accelerating |
| EPS — diluted (₹) [note C] | N/A | N/A | 8.68 (9M, not annualised) [2] | 8.23 [2] | Stable |
| CFO (₹ cr) | N/A | N/A | 8,547 [5] | 14,981 [5] | Accelerating |
| Capex — gross (₹ cr) [note D] | N/A | N/A | 1,663 [5] | 2,248 [5] | Stable |
| FCF — company-defined (₹ cr) | N/A | N/A | 5,880 [6] | 12,438 [6] | Accelerating |
| Working Capital (₹ cr) [note E] | N/A | N/A | (1,702) [7] | (7,395) [7] | Inflecting |
| Net Debt (₹ cr) — basic [note F] | N/A | N/A | 8,123 [7] | (2,082) [7] | Inflecting |
| Net Cash — company-defined (₹ cr) [note G] | N/A | N/A | (4,016) net debt [6] | 13,713 [6] | Inflecting |
| Net Debt / Underlying EBITDA (×) | N/A | N/A | 1.23 (9M EBITDA) | Net cash (negative ratio) | Inflecting |

**Notes on calculations:**

Note A — Gross Profit: Revenue from operations minus cost of materials consumed (including purchases and change in inventories). FY26: ₹83,855 − ₹56,732 = ₹27,123 cr. FY25 (9M): ₹58,217 − ₹39,196 = ₹19,021 cr. This is not a separately disclosed line in the filing; it is computed.

Note B — Underlying EBITDA and EBIT: disclosed explicitly by the company in the MD&A. Underlying EBITDA excludes fair-value loss on FVTPL equity investments (₹2,418 cr in FY26, relating to a subsidiary's investment portfolio), MTM on commodity/FX hedges, and exceptional items. FY23/FY24 underlying EBITDA margins sourced from CFO's transcript commentary (7.8% in FY23, expanding to 13.2% in FY26 over the three-year period).

Note C — EPS: Based on 3,682,331,373 fully diluted shares of ₹2 face value. FY25 EPS of ₹8.68 is annualised as stated in the filing; the period covers nine months.

Note D — Gross Capex: Sum of payments for PPE (₹1,321 cr) and other intangible assets (₹927 cr) from the cash flow statement. FY25 (9M): ₹830 cr PPE + ₹833 cr intangibles = ₹1,663 cr.

Note E — Working Capital: Current assets minus current liabilities (as per SEBI LODR definition; excludes current maturities of long-term debt and interest accrued). FY26: ₹23,208 − ₹30,603 = −₹7,395 cr (negative = supplier-funded). FY25: ₹23,969 − ₹25,671 = −₹1,702 cr.

Note F — Net Debt (basic): Total borrowings (non-current + current) minus cash and cash equivalents only. FY26: ₹4,817 − ₹6,899 = net cash ₹2,082 cr. FY25: ₹9,156 − ₹1,033 = net debt ₹8,123 cr.

Note G — Net Cash (company-defined): Includes lease liabilities and liquid investments (mutual funds, short-term deposits, Tata Capital investments). Company reports ₹13,713 cr net cash at FY26 end and ₹4,016 cr net debt at FY25 end.

Note on FY23/FY24 revenue estimates: CFO stated on the FQ4 2026 earnings call that revenue was "INR 77,000 crores for FY '26, up from INR 66,000 crores in FY '23" (standalone basis including Tata Cummins joint operation). These are standalone; the consolidated number for FY26 is ₹83,855 cr. The proportional scaling from standalone to consolidated implies FY23 consolidated-equivalent is approximately ₹66,000–68,000 cr. Treated as estimates; not from audited consolidated financial statements.

---

## 2. TTM Snapshot

TTM = four quarters ended March 31, 2026 (Q1 FY26 + Q2 FY26 + Q3 FY26 + Q4 FY26). This is the same as FY26 annual since the fiscal year ends March 31. The "Prior TTM" is not calculable because there is no Q1 FY25 data (the entity existed for only nine months in FY25). The closest comparison is the nine-month period from June 23 to March 31, 2025, which is not TTM-equivalent.

| Metric | FY26 TTM (= FY26 annual) | Prior TTM | Change | Evidence |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| Revenue (₹ cr) | 83,855 | N/A — no prior TTM | N/A | SEBI LODR Reg 33/52 Consolidated Audited Results, year ended Mar 31, 2026, filed May 13, 2026, Line I |
| Underlying EBITDA (₹ cr) | 10,314 | N/A | N/A | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), MD&A, p.215 |
| Underlying EBIT (₹ cr) | 8,538 | N/A | N/A | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), MD&A, p.215 |
| EPS diluted (₹) | 8.23 | N/A | N/A | SEBI LODR results filing, May 13, 2026, Note 2(xi) |
| CFO (₹ cr) | 14,981 | N/A | N/A | SEBI LODR results filing, May 13, 2026, Cash Flow Statement |
| Gross Capex (₹ cr) | 2,248 | N/A | N/A | SEBI LODR results filing, May 13, 2026, Cash Flow Statement |
| FCF — company-defined (₹ cr) | 12,438 | N/A | N/A | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), Value Creation Architecture, p.15 |
| Net debt at Mar 31, 2026 (basic, ₹ cr) | Net cash 2,082 | Net debt 8,123 at Mar 31, 2025 | Swing of ₹10,205 cr | SEBI LODR results filing, Balance Sheet, May 13, 2026 |

*Prior TTM: Not available. The entity was incorporated June 23, 2024 and has only nine months of prior-period data. Net debt comparison uses balance sheet point-in-time values for Mar 31, 2026 vs Mar 31, 2025.*

## 3. Latest Quarterly Trend Table

Quarterly data is available for four consolidated quarters. Q1 FY26 (April–June 2025) and Q2 FY26 (July–September 2025) are derived by subtraction: 9M FY26 consolidated revenue = ₹83,855 − Q4 ₹26,098 = ₹57,757 cr; Q3 = ₹21,847 cr; so H1 = ₹35,910 cr. Q2 standalone = ₹16,861 cr (from Q3 FY26 quarterly filing) and Q1 standalone = ₹15,682 cr. The consolidated H1 split is not separately disclosed; Q2 consolidated and Q1 consolidated are derived only at standalone level in the available filings. The table below presents four quarters of direct consolidated data where available, with Q1 and Q2 marked as derived/standalone where consolidated data is not separately stated.

Consolidated quarterly EBITDA margins: Q4 FY26 = 13.1% (per transcript), Q3 FY26 = 12.7% (per Q3 press release, CV segment), Q2 FY26 = ~10.4% (per Q3 press release reference), Q1 FY26 = inferred from 9M trend. YoY comparatives for Q3 and Q4 are available from the Q4 results filing (prior period = Q3 FY25, Q4 FY25 as re-presented unaudited numbers).

| Metric | Q1 FY26 | Q2 FY26 | Q3 FY26 | Q4 FY26 | QoQ Trend | YoY vs Same Q |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|---|
| Revenue — consolidated (₹ cr) | ~15,000–16,000 [8] | ~19,000–20,000 [8] | 21,847 [9] | 26,098 [2] | Accelerating H2 | Q3: +17% YoY [10]; Q4: +19% YoY [2] |
| Gross Margin % | N/A | N/A | N/A | N/A | N/A | N/A |
| Underlying EBITDA — consolidated (₹ cr) | ~1,900 [8] | ~2,300 [8] | 2,729 [10] | 3,388 [11] | Accelerating | Q3: +19% YoY [10]; Q4: +13% YoY est. |
| EBITDA Margin % — consolidated | ~12.0% est. [8] | ~12.5% est. [8] | 12.7% [10] | 13.1% [12] | Expanding | Q3: +30 bps YoY [10]; Q4: +220 bps YoY [12] |
| EPS diluted (₹) — consolidated | ~1.55 [8] | ~1.91 [9a] | 5.30 actual [13] | 5.69 actual [13] | Strong Q3-Q4 | Q3: beat consensus 4.47 by 18.6% [13]; Q4: beat consensus 5.20 by 10.7% [13] |

*Q1 and Q2 FY26 revenue and EBITDA are approximate, derived from 9M consolidated totals minus Q3 and Q4 disclosed figures, and calibrated against standalone quarterly results. Precise consolidated Q1/Q2 splits were not separately filed. EPS for Q1 is inferred; Q2 EPS uses the Q3 nine-month standalone figure (₹2.60 for 9M) as a guide. Q3 and Q4 are from audited/limited-review filings directly.*

*YoY comparatives: Q3 FY25 consolidated revenue = ₹21,863 cr (re-presented, unaudited) per the Q4 FY26 results filing. Q4 FY25 = ₹21,689 cr (re-presented, unaudited). These prior-period figures are not audited and are marked as re-presented in the filing.*

Partial-data note: Only four quarters of consolidated data exist for this entity (Q1–Q4 FY26). There is insufficient quarterly history to compute clean QoQ growth for Q1 and Q2. The trend table reflects the four available quarters; earlier quarterly data does not exist as audited consolidated TMCV financials.

## 4. Reported vs Adjusted Metrics

| Metric | Reported Value (FY26) | Adjusted/Underlying Value (FY26) | Adjustment Amount | Adjustment Reason | Evidence |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|---|
| EBITDA (₹ cr) | ~8,057 [note H] | 10,314 | +2,257 | Adds back fair-value loss on FVTPL equity investments (₹2,418 cr, non-cash, subsidiary investment portfolio); removes MTM FX/commodity hedges and certain other items | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), MD&A, p.215, footnote defining Underlying EBITDA |
| EBIT (₹ cr) | ~6,091 (PBT bei) | 8,538 | +2,447 | Same adjustments as EBITDA above, plus share of profit from equity-accounted investees (₹169 cr added) | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), MD&A, p.215; SEBI LODR results filing, Line VII |
| EPS diluted (₹) | 8.23 (reported PAT basis) | 15.91 [note I] | +7.68 | Normalised EPS per S&P Capital IQ consensus methodology — adjusts for exceptional items (₹1,428 cr loss) and FVTPL fair-value loss (₹2,418 cr, non-deductible) | FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript (S&P Capital IQ), p.1 — FY2026 consensus vs actual row |

Note H — Reported EBITDA computed: Profit before exceptional items and tax (₹6,091 cr) + Finance costs (₹874 cr) + Depreciation (₹1,945 cr) − Share of profit in equity accounted investees (₹169 cr) + FVTPL fair-value loss (₹2,418 cr) − Foreign exchange gain (₹100 cr) = approximately ₹11,059 cr. However the company uses a different "Operating Margin" definition in the filing (12.35% on revenue = ₹10,356 cr), adding back finance costs and FX but excluding FVTPL losses. The operating profit line as disclosed is ₹5,922 cr (profit before equity-accounted investees, exceptional items and tax). Reported EBITDA = ₹5,922 cr + ₹1,945 cr (D&A) + ₹874 cr (finance costs) − ₹100 cr (FX gain) = ₹8,641 cr reported EBITDA excluding FVTPL. The Underlying EBITDA of ₹10,314 cr additionally strips out product development expensed to P&L charged back, and FVTPL loss of ₹2,418 cr is excluded. The company does not define a single "reported EBITDA" line; the primary management metric is Underlying EBITDA.

Note I — Normalised EPS of ₹15.91 is the S&P Capital IQ consensus estimate for the full-year FY26 actual, which strips exceptional items and the non-cash FVTPL loss. This is a data-vendor adjusted figure, not a company-disclosed adjusted EPS.

---

## 5. Quarterly Seasonality Table

*Insufficient quarterly history for seasonality analysis.* This entity has only four quarters of consolidated financial history (Q1–Q4 FY26). A minimum of three fiscal years of quarterly data is required for a meaningful seasonality table. The prior-period nine-month data (June 23 to March 31, 2025) does not cover a full fiscal year and cannot be used to establish seasonality.

From the available single-year quarterly split, the revenue distribution in FY26 was approximately: Q1 ~18%, Q2 ~23%, Q3 ~26%, Q4 ~31%. This shows a pronounced H2-heavy pattern (Q4 being the strongest quarter), consistent with infrastructure spending seasonality and festive-season freight demand in India. However, one year is not sufficient to confirm this as a structural pattern vs. a cycle-specific effect (the FY26 Q4 was boosted by recovery from H1 supply-chain disruptions and GST 2.0 rollout effects).

---

## 6. Key Trend Summary

Revenue is growing. On a like-for-like full-year basis, revenue grew from approximately ₹66,000–68,000 cr in FY23 to ₹83,855 cr in FY26 — a three-year CAGR of roughly 7–8% — with the pace re-accelerating in FY26 (+9.8% over the annualised FY25 level per the Chairman's letter). The FY26 revenue of ₹83,855 cr is the highest in the company's history as a CV-focused entity.

Margins are expanding, rapidly and across multiple years. Underlying EBITDA margin widened from approximately 7.8% in FY23 to 12.3% in FY26 — a 550 basis-point structural expansion over three years, confirmed by management in the earnings call. This is not a one-quarter effect. The 11th consecutive quarter of double-digit EBITDA margins was delivered in Q4 FY26. EBIT margin crossed 10% for the first time in FY26, reaching 10.2%.

Seasonality cannot yet be confirmed from one year of data. The FY26 quarterly pattern is heavily H2-weighted (Q4 = ~31% of annual revenue). This is consistent with India's broader infrastructure and freight cycle but must be treated as indicative, not established seasonal fact, until at least two more full years of data are available.

The single biggest inflection in the last three years is the balance sheet transformation: from a net debt position of approximately ₹4,016 cr at the end of the nine-month FY25 period to net cash of ₹13,713 cr (company-defined) at March 31, 2026 — a swing of roughly ₹17,700 cr in one full year, driven by ₹14,981 cr of operating cash flow and disciplined repayment of ₹3,491 cr of long-term borrowings plus ₹963 cr of short-term borrowings. The company moved from net-debt to net-cash in a single year, which substantially changes its financial risk profile going forward.

---

Consolidated Q3 FY26 revenue = ₹21,847 cr (higher than segment due to insurance broking subsidiary). EBITDA ₹2,729 cr computed: ₹21,847 cr × 12.7% ≈ ₹2,774 cr (at consolidated level using the stated consolidated margin of 12.5% per Q3 press release = ₹2,731 cr).

[12] FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript (S&P Capital IQ, May 13, 2026), CFO presentation, p.5: "EBITDA margin expanded to 12.1%, up 220 bps Y-o-Y" (standalone); consolidated margin 13.1% per same page. Q4 FY25 consolidated EBITDA margin computed from re-presented numbers: Q4 FY25 revenue ₹21,863 cr, Q4 FY25 operating profit per results filing ~11% margin = ~₹2,405 cr (consistent with FY25 operating margin of 13.21% annualised, noting Q4 FY25 was lower at ~10.9%).

[13] FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript (S&P Capital IQ, May 13, 2026), Cover page: Q3 FY26 EPS actual = ₹5.30 vs consensus ₹4.47 (+18.57% surprise); Q4 FY26 EPS actual = ₹5.69 vs consensus ₹5.20 (+10.70% surprise). Full-year FY26 EPS normalised actual = ₹15.91 vs consensus ₹17.82 (−16.72% surprise — below normalised consensus due to FVTPL investment losses being non-deductible, driving a higher-than-expected tax rate per the filing Note 9).


---
## earnings/02_revenue-drivers.md

# Revenue Drivers — TMCV (Tata Motors Limited, formerly TML Commercial Vehicles Limited)

**Reporting regime:** India / SEBI-LODR. Reporting standard: Ind AS. Currency: INR (₹ crores). Fiscal year ends March 31. FY26 (April 1, 2025 to March 31, 2026) is the first full twelve-month year for this entity.

---

## 1. Segment Decomposition Status

Segment decomposition applied — 2 segments from business-model module (03_segment-map.md).

However, the company is effectively a single-segment business: the Commercial Vehicle (CV) segment contributes 98.5% of revenue (₹82,611 cr of ₹83,855 cr total in FY26) and over 100% of operating segment profit. The Others segment (insurance broking) contributes 1.2% and is operationally dependent on the parent's vehicle sales ecosystem for its volumes. The analysis proceeds at consolidated level with CV-segment breakdowns by product line where disclosed. Segment-level EBITDA is not separately disclosed in the filings (only Underlying EBITDA at consolidated level); segment results use the CODM operating-profit equivalent metric.

---

## 2. Revenue Driver Tree

TMCV is a commercial vehicle manufacturer. The applicable formula:

**Revenue = Units wholesaled × Average Selling Price (ASP) + Non-cyclical / downstream revenue (parts, services, digital, Smart City)**

More precisely at the product-line level:
- **Vehicle revenue** = Σ (Volume by product line × ASP by product line)
- **Non-vehicle revenue** = Parts & Services + Smart City Mobility + Fleet Edge subscriptions + Insurance Broking (Others segment)

In FY26, vehicle revenue (HCV ₹37,199 cr + ILMCV ₹11,212 cr + SCV/Pickup ₹7,558 cr + CV Passenger ₹8,807 cr = ₹64,776 cr) accounted for approximately 77% of CV segment revenue; the balance reflects spares, services, and associated revenue. The non-cyclical revenue stream is growing faster than vehicles (management cited noncyclical CAGR running at 2.7x the cyclical business over the multi-year period per FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, May 13, 2026, p.4).

[Source: FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), MD&A, p.213–216; FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, S&P Capital IQ, May 13, 2026, p.4]

---

## 3. Market / Share / Price / Mix Split

| Driver Bucket | Current Direction | Evidence | Importance /100 |
|---|---|---|---:|
| End-market demand (India CV industry) | Improving — industry TIV reached highest-ever 10.79 lakh units in FY26, +12.6% YoY, surpassing the FY19 peak; April 2026 e-way bill generation +12% YoY confirming early FY27 momentum | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), Operating Context, p.24; FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, May 13, 2026, p.6 | 40 |
| Company market share | Improving — TMCV outpaced industry in FY26 (+14% volume vs industry +12.5%); HCV market share reached highest in a decade at 55% offtake in FY26; VAHAN share gains in vans and SCV pickup | FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, May 13, 2026, p.6; FQ3 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, Jan 29, 2026, p.6 | 25 |
| Price / realization (ASP) | Stable to improving at segment level, but headline consolidated ASP is declining due to product mix (vans growing as a share of volume, pulling average down); truck ASPs improving YoY; 1–2% selective price increases taken in Jan 2026 and Apr 2026 to offset commodity cost; Q4 FY26 standalone revenue ₹24,500 cr at +22% YoY "due to ASP improvement driven by pricing discipline and favorable mix" | FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, May 13, 2026, p.5; FQ3 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, Jan 29, 2026, Q&A p.8 | 20 |
| Product / customer / geography mix | Mixed — within vehicles, mix is shifting toward higher-value HCV and tipper (positive), but van volumes growing faster pulls blended ASP lower; non-cyclical downstream revenue (parts, services, Fleet Edge) growing at noncyclical CAGR 2.7x cyclical, adding high-margin revenue; export mix improving (+54% YoY in FY26); electric buses low-margin or negative in early deployments | FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, May 13, 2026, p.4–6; FQ3 2026 Earnings Call Q&A, Jan 29, 2026, p.8 | 20 |
| FX translation | Immaterial — TMCV reports in INR; export revenue (~7–8% of total, inference from volume data) is transaction-based not translation-based; FX impact on reported consolidated revenue is negligible | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), MD&A, p.215–216 | 2 |
| M&A / divestitures | Potential future impact: Iveco acquisition (announced July 2025, ~€3.8 billion, ~140,000 vehicles/year, revenue ~€13.4 billion in 2025) expected to close Q2 FY27; if consolidated, would roughly double revenue scale; NOT yet reflected in FY26 numbers | FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, May 13, 2026, p.4; SEBI LODR results filing, May 13, 2026, Note 7 | Not in current revenue; transformative if closed |

Note: Growth in FY26 is organic — no material acquisitions were consolidated. The Iveco acquisition had not closed by March 31, 2026. International Business organic volume growth of 54% in FY26 was market-driven, not acquisition-driven.

---

## 4. Revenue Driver Table (Consolidated)

| Driver | Current Level | Direction | Magnitude | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Domestic CV industry demand (freight activity) | 10.79 lakh industry units in FY26, highest ever; freight index rising; e-way bills +12–19% YoY through Q4 FY26 and early FY27 | Improving — structurally supported by India infrastructure capex (Union Budget FY27 capex ₹12.22 lakh cr), GST 2.0 consumption recovery; near-term risk from Operation Sindoor sentiment and early monsoon in H1 FY27 | High | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), Operating Context p.24; FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, May 13, 2026, p.6 |
| TMCV wholesale volumes | 435,227 units FY26 (consolidated), +14% YoY (excluding Tata Daewoo Mobility); Q4 FY26 wholesale 131,800 units, +25% YoY | Improving — management guides single-digit growth for Q1 FY27 given geopolitical uncertainty; base effect from strong H2 FY26 moderates YoY comparison going into H1 FY27 | High | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), KPI highlights p.28; FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, May 13, 2026, p.5 |
| HCV / tipper demand | HCV highest offtake market share in a decade (55%) in FY26; tipper demand driven by infrastructure activity and urban construction; replacement cycle active (40% of volume from fleet owners replacing 4–6 year-old trucks, per management) | Improving — infrastructure spending sustained; tippers strong post-monsoon; seasonal low during Q1 FY27 monsoon period | High | FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, May 13, 2026, p.6; FQ3 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, Jan 29, 2026, p.10–11 |
| ILMCV and SCV/Pickup volumes | ILMCV +26% in Q3 FY26 YoY, +15% in Q2 FY26 YoY; SCV/Pickup growth driven by Ace Pro launch and Intra EV; highest growth rates among product lines in FY26 | Improving — GST 2.0 boosted B2C demand; new launches (Ace Pro EV, Intra EV) supporting further volume growth; EV penetration in SCV pickup reaching 7% | FQ3 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, Jan 29, 2026, p.6; FQ2 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, Nov 13, 2025, p.4 | High |
| Average selling price (ASP) / revenue per unit | Standalone Q4 FY26 revenue per unit improving (pricing discipline); but consolidated blended ASP declining because vans (lower ASP) and absence of high-ASP electric buses are diluting the mix; 1% price increase Jan 2026, 2% price increase Apr 2026 taken | Stable — selective price increases partially offset commodity inflation; full pass-through unlikely given competitive pressure; management stated explicitly it would not pass full commodity increase to protect demand momentum | Mid | FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, May 13, 2026, Q&A p.8–9; FQ3 2026 Earnings Call Transcript Q&A, Jan 29, 2026, p.8 |
| Non-cyclical downstream revenue (parts, services, Fleet Edge, Smart City) | Non-cyclical revenue growing at 18.2% in FY26 (parts and services); Fleet Edge >1 million connected vehicles; Smart City 3,815 electric buses deployed; Fleet Edge subscription renewals doubled Q1 to Q4 FY26 | Improving — structural multi-year growth trajectory; non-cyclical revenue now a "healthy double digit" percentage of total revenue per management; provides resilience through CV cycles | Mid | FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, May 13, 2026, p.4–7; FQ2 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, Nov 13, 2025, p.5 |
| International / export volumes | 54% YoY growth in FY26 (full year); Indonesia order (70,000 units, first shipments already dispatched as of May 2026); SAP countries (Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, SAARC) recovering; Middle East / North Africa shipments paused last 2 months due to West Asia conflict | Improving underlying, but near-term risk from West Asia geopolitical disruption; Indonesia order represents a firm multi-quarter revenue tailwind; management recalibrating MENA exposure | Mid | FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, May 13, 2026, p.6, Q&A p.11; FQ3 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, Jan 29, 2026, Q&A p.12 |
| Fleet operator (transporter) profitability and buying confidence | Transporter profitability "resilient" per management tracking ~1 million Fleet Edge vehicles; delinquency trends stabilizing; freight rates up 2–5% post-GST 2.0; diesel price the single biggest cost variable (30–50% of total cost of ownership) | Stable to improving — but diesel price increases could compress transporter margins and delay purchase decisions; HCV fleet owners at ~80% cargo utilization (approaching addition-of-capacity threshold) | High | FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, May 13, 2026, p.6; FQ3 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, Jan 29, 2026, Q&A p.11 |
| Government infrastructure capex and bus tenders | Union Budget FY27 capex ₹12.22 lakh cr (+9% over FY26 ₹11.21 lakh cr); TMCV has 6,000+ government bus tender wins as of Q3 FY26, deliveries now commencing; electric bus tender pipeline (CESL, PM E-DRIVE) large but TMCV being selective on payment security terms | Improving — infrastructure capex growing; bus order book visible for next 10–12 months; electric bus tender participation cautious (prudent given payment security concerns, management stated TMCV was not L1 in recent tenders) | Mid | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), Operating Context p.24; FQ3 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, Jan 29, 2026, Q&A p.10 |
| Diesel price (external monitorable) | Diesel prices "key monitorable" per management; not under company control; elevated diesel prices reduce transporter profitability and can defer purchase decisions, especially for HCV fleet owners | Deteriorating risk — commodity and geopolitical factors pushing fuel costs higher; West Asia crisis a risk factor; management has explicitly called this the first immediate challenge for FY27 | High | FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, May 13, 2026, p.7, Q&A p.8; FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), External Environment p.23 |
| New product launches | 17 next-generation trucks launched at start of Q4 FY26; Azura series (ILMCV platform); Ace Pro and Ace Pro EV; Intra EV Pickup; 5–7 electric trucks across 7–55 tonne range; Winger 9S; upgrades across full truck range to European safety norms | Improving — product portfolio refresh broadest in several years; higher-payload variants addressing specific duty cycles; EV range enables participation in electric bus market and fleet electrification; launches support market share and ASP improvement | Mid | FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, May 13, 2026, p.4; FQ3 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, Jan 29, 2026, p.4–5; FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), Our Offerings p.18 |

---

## 5. Revenue Drivers By Segment

### Segment: Commercial Vehicle (98.5% of revenue, ₹82,611 cr FY26)

This segment encompasses all four product lines. Sub-segment revenue and volume data below is from MD&A product-line disclosures; these are not separately reported Ind AS 108 segments.

| Driver | Current Level | Direction | Magnitude | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| HCV (Heavy Commercial Vehicles) — 29.5% of volume, ₹37,199 cr revenue | 128,292 units FY26; highest market share in decade; tippers strong post-monsoon; replacement demand active | Improving — but Q1 FY27 cautious due to seasonal monsoon + diesel price uncertainty | High | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), MD&A p.213–216 |
| ILMCV (Intermediate / Light Medium, 5–19 tonnes) — 19.8% of volume, ₹11,212 cr revenue | 86,224 units FY26; +26% YoY in Q3 FY26; Azura series launched; new Azura platform improving applicability | Improving — strongest growth sub-segment; GST 2.0 boosted light freight demand; e-commerce logistics driving sustained demand | High | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), MD&A p.213; FQ3 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, Jan 29, 2026, p.5 |
| SCV / Pickup — 36.6% of volume, ₹7,558 cr revenue | 159,398 units FY26; largest share of volume; Ace Pro launch; EV penetration growing to 7%; last-mile and FMCG demand | Improving — Ace Pro EV and Intra EV adding volume; financier (NBFC) asset quality improving for these vehicles; penetration still low | Mid | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), MD&A p.213; FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, May 13, 2026, p.6 |
| CV Passenger (buses and vans) — 14.1% of volume, ₹8,807 cr revenue | 61,313 units FY26; 6,000+ government bus tender wins; Smart City 3,815 electric buses; private MCV bus growing | Improving — government tender pipeline converting to revenue; electric bus selective participation; private bus demand steady | Mid | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), MD&A p.213; FQ3 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, Jan 29, 2026, Q&A p.10 |
| Parts, Services, and non-vehicle (within CV segment) | Non-cyclical revenue +18.2% YoY; Fleet Edge subscriptions doubling; e-Dukaan, FleetVerse growing | Improving — structural, multi-year growth; provides revenue base resilience independent of CV cycle | Mid | FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, May 13, 2026, p.4–5; FQ2 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, Nov 13, 2025, p.5–6 |
| International / exports (within CV segment) | +54% YoY FY26; +17% Q4 FY26; Indonesia 70,000-unit order in delivery; Sri Lanka, SAARC recovering | Improving underlying, near-term West Asia disruption a risk | Mid | FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, May 13, 2026, p.5; FQ3 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, Jan 29, 2026, p.7 |

### Segment: Others / Insurance Broking (1.2% of revenue, ₹968 cr FY26)

This segment is structurally dependent on TMCV vehicle sales volumes. As vehicle sales grow, insurance attachment grows proportionally. No independent revenue drivers apply. Margin is high (13.9% operating margin in FY26) but absolute revenue is immaterial to consolidated analysis.

[Source: SEBI LODR Reg 33/52 Consolidated Audited Results, Year ended March 31, 2026, May 13, 2026, p.4 — Segment Results table]

---

## 6. Revenue Growth Decomposition

The most recent full-year revenue growth is FY26 vs annualised FY25 (management's stated basis: +9.8% from ₹76,359 cr to ₹83,855 cr per Chairman's letter). For a quarterly read, Q4 FY26 consolidated revenue was ₹26,098 cr vs Q4 FY25 ₹21,689 cr = +20.3% YoY (using re-presented unaudited Q4 FY25 figure from the results filing).

**Full-year FY26 decomposition (consolidated, management standalone stated as illustration):**

Per CFO's FQ4 2026 presentation (standalone basis, Tata Cummins included): "Revenue came in at INR 77,000 crores for FY '26, up from INR 66,000 crores in FY '23." On the full-year +9.8% growth (consolidated), management attributed the drivers to: "higher volumes," "better realization," "favorable mix," and "growing contribution of downstream and services businesses." No company-disclosed arithmetic breakdown of volume vs price vs mix vs FX contributions was provided for the consolidated full year.

Based on available data, a partial inference-based decomposition is constructed below. Components marked "Inference" are not from filings.

| Component | Contribution to Growth (pp) | Evidence |
|---|---:|---|
| Volume (wholesale units +14% YoY, i.e., 435,227 vs ~382,000 prior) | ~+9 to +10 pp | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), KPI p.28; FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, May 13, 2026, p.5 — "total volume stood at 428,000 units [standalone], registering a 14% increase Y-o-Y" |
| Price / realization (selective increases partially offset by mix dilution from vans) | ~0 to +1 pp (Inference — net effect estimated to be modest given offsetting van mix) | FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, May 13, 2026, Q&A — management stated ASPs declining overall but improving within truck segment; Q4 standalone +22% YoY attributed to "ASP improvement" |
| Product mix (non-cyclical downstream growing faster, HCV mix improvement vs SCV/van dilution) | ~+1 to +2 pp (Inference) | FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, May 13, 2026, p.4 — noncyclical CAGR at 2.7x cyclical |
| FX translation | ~0 pp | Not material; TMCV reports in INR, negligible translation exposure |
| Acquisitions / divestitures | 0 pp | No acquisitions consolidated in FY26; Iveco acquisition not yet closed |
| International business (organic export growth) | Included in volume and mix above; export volumes +54% YoY are organic | FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, May 13, 2026, p.5 |
| **Total revenue growth (consolidated, management-stated basis)** | **+9.8%** | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), Chairman's Message p.8 |

**Limitation:** TMCV does not disclose a formal price/volume/mix bridge for consolidated revenue. The volume contribution above uses unit wholesale data; the implied revenue-per-unit change is derived by difference and is labeled inference. A cleaner decomposition requires revenue-per-unit by product line across two comparable full years, which is not available given FY25 is only nine months of audited data.

---

## 7. The Single Biggest Revenue Driver

**Domestic India CV industry volume demand, shaped by transporter profitability and freight activity.**

TMCV earns approximately 77% of its revenue from vehicle sales, and vehicle sales are priced per unit. The company holds roughly 39–40% of the domestic CV market by wholesale volume. That means a 10% shift in industry demand volumes — roughly 1.08 lakh units on a base of 10.79 lakh — would, all else equal, move TMCV's consolidated revenue by approximately ₹7,000–8,000 crore (8–10% of FY26 revenue), assuming blended ASP of roughly ₹14–15 lakh per vehicle (inference from ₹83,855 cr revenue / 435,227 units, blended across vehicle and non-vehicle streams at the consolidated level — actual per-vehicle ASP is higher once non-vehicle revenue is excluded).

The direction of this driver right now is cautiously improving but fragile. The underlying freight fundamentals — e-way bill generation growing, transporter fleet utilization at healthy levels, infrastructure capex budgeted up — all support continued demand. However, diesel price is the direct variable that can break this: management explicitly flagged diesel as having "30–50% impact on total cost of ownership" and stated it will "remain a very, very key monitorable." If diesel prices rise materially in FY27, transporter profitability would compress, fleet purchase decisions would be deferred, and H1 FY27 demand (already seasonally soft due to monsoon) could undershoot the single-digit growth management has tentatively guided for Q1 FY27.

The Indonesia export order (70,000 units over multiple quarters beginning FY27) provides a meaningful volume buffer — at an inferred average export ASP, this could add ₹2,000–3,500 cr of revenue in FY27 (Inference: based on ₹3,000–5,000 per unit export ASP discount to domestic, applied to 70,000 units spread over 4–6 quarters). This partially de-risks revenue from a domestic demand softening.

[Evidence: FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, May 13, 2026, p.6–7, Q&A p.8–9; FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), External Environment p.22–23; FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript, Q&A p.8 — "diesel will have at least 30% to 50% impact on the total cost of ownership"]


---
## earnings/03_margin-drivers.md

# Margin Drivers — TMCV (Tata Motors Limited, formerly TML Commercial Vehicles Limited)

**Reporting regime:** India / SEBI-LODR. Reporting standard: Ind AS. Currency: INR (₹ crores). Fiscal year ends March 31. FY26 = full year ended March 31, 2026. FY25 comparative = nine months only (June 23, 2024 to March 31, 2025) — not directly comparable. Transcript available: FQ4 2026 earnings call, May 13, 2026 (S&P Capital IQ).

---

## 1. Segment Decomposition Status

TMCV is a single-segment business for practical purposes. The Commercial Vehicle segment contributes 98.5% of revenue (₹82,611 cr of ₹83,855 cr total) and over 100% of operating segment profit (₹8,727 cr of ₹8,414 cr total net segment results, with the Corporate/Unallocable drag of −₹448 cr pulling the total below the CV segment alone). The Others segment (insurance broking) contributes 1.2% of revenue and 1.6% of profit. The CODM reviews the automotive operations as one segment under Ind AS 108.

Segment-level EBITDA and capex are not disclosed — only segment results at the EBIT-equivalent level. Underlying EBITDA (₹10,314 cr, 12.3% margin) and Underlying EBIT (₹8,538 cr, 10.2% margin) are disclosed at consolidated level only.

**Conclusion:** Segment-level margin decomposition beyond the consolidated CV segment is not possible from disclosed data. All analysis proceeds at the consolidated CV-group level. The business-model segment-map (analyses/TMCV_2026-06-07/business-model/03_segment-map.md) confirms this assessment.

Within the CV segment, management discloses product-line revenue (HCV ₹37,199 cr, ILMCV ₹11,212 cr, CV Passenger ₹8,807 cr, SCV/Pickup ₹7,558 cr) but not product-line profitability. Product-mix within the CV segment is therefore treated as a driver but its margin impact per product line cannot be precisely quantified.

---

## 2. Cost Stack

All figures are for FY26 (year ended March 31, 2026), consolidated Ind AS. FY25 figures are for the nine-month period (June 23, 2024 to March 31, 2025) and are not directly comparable — percentage-of-revenue comparisons are used where meaningful.

| Cost Line | FY26 Amount (₹ cr) | % of Revenue (FY26) | % of Revenue (FY25, 9M) | Direction vs FY25 | Margin Risk |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|---|
| Cost of materials consumed (incl. purchases & inventory change) | 56,732 | 67.7% | 67.3% | Stable / very slight increase | High — largest cost line; no contractual pass-through |
| Employee cost | 5,804 | 6.9% | 7.3% | Improving (−40 bps) | Low-Mid — operating leverage pulling this down |
| Other expenses (freight, warranty, works ops, IT, power/fuel, etc.) | 11,689 | 13.9% | 14.9% | Improving (−100 bps) | Mid — warranty and freight are watch items |
| Product development / engineering expenses (P&L) | 789 | 0.9% | 1.4% | Improving (−50 bps) | Low — guided to stay 2–4% of revenue on total R&D |
| Depreciation and amortisation | 1,945 | 2.3% | 2.9% | Improving (−60 bps) | Low — reduced due to amortisation completion of certain assets |
| Finance costs (interest expense) | 874 | 1.0% | 1.9% | Strongly improving (−90 bps) | Low — debt nearly eliminated; net cash position |
| Fair-value loss on FVTPL equity investments | 2,418 | 2.9% | 0.0% | Large FY26 charge (non-cash) | Low on recurring basis — non-cash, non-operating, strips out of Underlying EBITDA |
| Foreign exchange (gain)/loss (net) | (100) | (0.1%) | 0.2% | Gain in FY26 | Low |

**Sub-components of Other Expenses (₹ cr, FY26 / FY25 9M):**
- Works operation and other expenses: ₹4,266 / ₹3,140
- Warranty charges: ₹2,230 / ₹1,699
- Freight, transportation, port charges: ₹1,373 / ₹936
- Processing charges: ₹1,205 / ₹897
- IT/computer expenses: ₹920 / ₹823
- Power and fuel: ₹627 / ₹457
- Stores, spare parts and tools: ₹568 / ₹343
- Publicity: ₹381 / ₹323
- MTM (gain)/loss on commodity derivatives: ₹69 / ₹5

[Source: FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), MD&A, p.216–217, consolidated income statement and cost breakdowns]

**Notes on what is not separately disclosed:** Raw material sub-components (steel, aluminium, copper) are not individually quantified in the financial statements. Energy cost is embedded in "Power and fuel" (₹627 cr = 0.7% of revenue) and in materials. Freight is separately identified (₹1,373 cr = 1.6% of revenue). No separate R&D expense line beyond the capitalized/expensed split mentioned above.

---

## 3. Gross Margin → EBITDA Margin → EBIT Margin Walk

*Gross profit defined as: Revenue from operations minus cost of materials consumed (including purchases and inventory changes). This is a computed line, not separately disclosed in the filing.*

| Margin Level | FY26 | FY25 (9M) | Change (bps) | Main Reason | Evidence |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|---|
| Gross margin | 32.3% | 32.7% | −40 bps | Material costs held broadly flat at 67.7% vs 67.3% of revenue; slight compression from commodity cost increases partially offset by VAVE savings and better realisation | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), MD&A, p.214–216: "Material costs, as a percentage of revenue, broadly remained in line with the previous year despite an increase in input costs, supported by improved realisations and operational efficiencies." |
| Underlying EBITDA margin | 12.3% | 11.3% | +100 bps | SG&A-equivalent and other expenses fell as % of revenue (operating leverage); employee cost leverage (−40 bps); R&D charged to P&L fell (−50 bps); finance cost not in EBITDA. Underlying EBITDA also strips FVTPL loss. | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), MD&A, p.215; FQ4 2026 Earnings Call, May 13, 2026, G.V. Ramanan: "EBITDA margins have expanded from 7.8% in '23, 13.2% in FY '26. That's nearly 550 bps of structural improvement over 3 years." |
| Underlying EBIT margin | 10.2% | 8.6% | +160 bps | D&A as % of revenue fell (−60 bps) on top of EBITDA expansion; share of profit from equity-accounted investees (₹169 cr) included in Underlying EBIT. EBIT crossed double-digit for first time. | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), MD&A, p.215: "Underlying EBIT FY26 = ₹8,538 cr (10.2%)" |

**Pass-through lag:** Price increases lag input cost increases by approximately one quarter, and the pass-through is partial. When Q4 FY26 saw a 100 basis point variable cost impact from elevated steel, aluminium, and copper prices, the company took a 2% price increase in April 2026 (for Q1 FY27) but explicitly chose not to pass on the full amount. Management stated: "we have decided to not pass on the entire commodity increases... we don't want to impact the demand momentum." [FQ4 2026 Earnings Call, May 13, 2026, Girish Wagh, Q&A] This implies the pass-through covers roughly half the commodity shock in any given quarter, with the balance absorbed through Value Analysis and Value Engineering (VAVE). The business-model value-chain output (analyses/TMCV_2026-06-07/business-model/06_value-chain.md) characterises this as 40–60% pass-through, ~1 quarter lag.

---

## 4. Margin Walk — Which Margin Level Matters Most?

For TMCV, **Underlying EBITDA margin** is the primary metric to track.

The reason: TMCV is a capital-intensive manufacturer with significant depreciation from 11 manufacturing facilities and ongoing product development capitalisation. D&A is real and recurring but can vary quarter to quarter based on asset completion timing (D&A fell in FY26 partly due to amortisation completion for some assets — a non-recurring tailwind). Finance costs have collapsed with the debt paydown (from 1.9% of revenue in the nine-month FY25 to 1.0% in FY26) and are now approaching zero as the company moves to net cash; this makes reported EBIT and PBT less useful as comparators going forward since the interest benefit is fading. Reported EBITDA is distorted by the large non-cash FVTPL fair-value loss on a subsidiary's equity investment portfolio (₹2,418 cr in FY26, nil in FY25 nine-month). Underlying EBITDA, which strips these non-operating items, is the metric management guides to, analysts ask about, and which best reflects operating performance.

EBIT margin is also important but Underlying EBIT is a better version of it than reported EBIT, for the same reasons. Gross margin is a useful secondary check on commodity pass-through dynamics but the company's operating leverage story plays out below the gross line.

---

## 5. Margin Driver Table (Consolidated)

| Driver | Impact on Margins | Direction | Magnitude | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Input commodity costs (steel, aluminium, copper, precious metals) | Raises or compresses gross margin directly; 67.7% of revenue is material cost | Headwind — management confirmed "significantly higher" Q1 FY27 commodity pressure vs Q4 FY26 100 bps impact; steel prices specifically cited as rising | High — 100 bps gross margin compression per the Q4 FY26 experience of a moderate commodity spike; sustained inflation without full pass-through could compress 150–200 bps | FQ4 2026 Earnings Call, May 13, 2026, Girish Wagh: "commodity bids are certainly serious. And we've already seen around 100-basis point impact in Q4. Beyond the impact in Q4, we have also seen significantly higher impact being seen in Q1." K.M. Balaji (Ashok Leyland CFO, same sector context): "There has been a significant increase... predominantly steel." |
| Pricing / volume realisation | Offsets commodity costs; higher ASPs in trucks segment (management confirmed segment-level ASP is rising in trucks on YoY basis) | Tailwind — truck ASPs rising YoY; 2% price increase taken April 2026; blended ASP drag from van/e-bus mix | Mid-High — management framed price + volume mix as contributing "nearly 250 bps" of EBIT margin improvement in Q4 FY26 alone; pricing tailwind real but partial | FQ4 2026 Earnings Call, May 13, 2026, G.V. Ramanan: "Higher volume mix, price realization together contributed nearly 250 basis points of EBIT margin improvement." Girish Wagh: "in trucks, there is a healthy increase in ASPs on a Y-o-Y basis." |
| Operating leverage / volume growth | Fixed costs spread over higher volume lowers per-unit cost; employee cost and other expenses as % of revenue fell as volumes grew 14% YoY | Tailwind — volumes grew 14% to 435,227 units in FY26; management guided single-digit H1 FY27 growth but guided "quarter by quarter" | High — the 550 bps EBITDA expansion over FY23–FY26 is substantially explained by operating leverage on revenue scaling from ~₹66,000 cr to ₹83,855 cr | FQ4 2026 Earnings Call, May 13, 2026, G.V. Ramanan: "PBT before exceptional items stood at INR 8,700 crores... This is the result of operating leverage playing out as we scale, combined with cost discipline, improved realization." |
| Product / segment mix (noncyclical vs cyclical) | Noncyclical revenue (spare parts, aftermarket, services, Fleet Edge subscriptions) carries higher and more stable margins vs cyclical vehicle sales | Tailwind — noncyclical CAGR running at 2.7× cyclical CAGR per management; noncyclical delivered 18.2% growth in FY26 | Mid — noncyclical is a growing share but still a minority of total revenue; its expansion contributes to margin mix improvement structurally | FQ4 2026 Earnings Call, May 13, 2026, G.V. Ramanan: "noncyclical CAGR running at 2.7x a cyclical CAGR." FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), Chairman's Letter, p.9: "Non cyclical segment delivered strong growth of 18.2%." |
| Product mix within CV (HCV vs SCV vs EV vs ICV) | HCV carries higher realisation; EV buses historically boosted ASPs but were mostly absent in FY26 after FAME incentive discontinuation | Mixed/headwind in FY26 — EV bus volumes essentially absent from FY26 (ASP drag confirmed by management); van mix increase also diluted blended ASP | Mid — management explicitly flagged this as the reason blended ASP fell at consolidated level despite per-segment ASP rising | FQ4 2026 Earnings Call, May 13, 2026, Girish Wagh: "The key reason for this drop [in blended ASP] is essentially due to the increase in number of vans in our portfolio in the volumes. And compared to last year, we hardly have any electric buses being sold this year. This alone is one very important reason." |
| VAVE (Value Analysis and Value Engineering) savings | Structural cost engineering that reduces per-unit material cost; offsets commodity inflation | Tailwind — institutionalised programme, continuously running; absorbed part of FY26 and Q4 FY26 commodity spike | Mid — management cites VAVE as covering the portion of commodity inflation not passed through pricing | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), Risk Management, p.40: "VAVE is an institutionalised and continuous programme focused on improving product cost structures." |
| Finance costs / interest | Interest was 1.9% of revenue in the nine-month FY25; fell to 1.0% in FY26; approaching near-zero as the company is now net cash | Tailwind — but this is now largely played out; the company is net cash, so further debt paydown benefit is minimal | Low — the step-down from 1.9% to 1.0% of revenue contributed ~90 bps to reported EBIT margin improvement; further compression is limited as net debt is essentially zero | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), MD&A, p.217: "Finance Costs ₹874 cr FY26 vs ₹1,079 cr FY25 (9M). Our net interest expense improved in FY26 as a percentage of total revenues. This improvement was primarily driven by a reduction in borrowings." |
| D&A trends | Depreciation fell as a % of revenue in FY26 partly due to amortisation completion on certain assets — a partially non-recurring tailwind | Tailwind in FY26 but not fully structural — new capex of ~₹2,800 cr/year (management guided FY27 similar) will add D&A going forward | Low-Mid — D&A was 2.3% of revenue in FY26 vs 2.9% in FY25 nine months; going forward expect to stabilise as new capex adds D&A but revenue grows | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), MD&A, p.217: "In FY26, Depreciation and amortization expenses declined, primarily due to the completion of the amortization period of certain assets." |
| Warranty charges | Warranty was ₹2,230 cr (2.7% of revenue) in FY26 vs ₹1,699 cr in nine-month FY25 (2.9%); slight improvement as % of revenue | Neutral / improving — management focuses on quality; new product launches (17 next-gen trucks in FY26) carry some warranty risk | Low-Mid — absolute amount growing with volumes; percentage is stable; any quality issue on new platforms is a risk | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), MD&A, p.216: Warranty charges ₹2,230 cr FY26 vs ₹1,699 cr FY25 (9M). |
| Iveco acquisition integration costs | Proposed acquisition of Iveco Group at EUR 3.8 billion (~₹41,691 cr) expected to close Q2 FY27; will bring ~140,000 vehicles/year and EUR 13.4 bn revenue; integration involves transaction costs, financing costs on EUR 3.8 bn facility | Headwind — deal closure will introduce acquisition-related exceptional items; EUR 3.8 bn loan facility will raise finance costs significantly | High (if deal closes) — EUR 3.8 bn bridge loan at current rates implies ~₹3,000–4,000 cr annual interest in the near term, which would reverse the finance cost improvement and could compress reported EBIT margin by ~3–4% of revenue | FQ4 2026 Earnings Call, May 13, 2026, G.V. Ramanan: "we expect this to kind of spill into Q2"; FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), Board's Report, p.116: acquisition at EUR 14.1/share, total ~EUR 3.8 billion. |
| Diesel price / freight availability (macro input to demand) | Diesel is the largest operating cost for transport customers; elevated diesel prices reduce fleet operator profitability and defer replacement demand, indirectly pressuring TMCV volumes and hence operating leverage | Headwind — diesel prices elevated; West Asia geopolitical tensions adding uncertainty; management flagged diesel as "very, very key monitorable" entering FY27 | Mid — indirect effect through demand and volume, not direct cost to TMCV's P&L; but volume is TMCV's most powerful operating lever | FQ4 2026 Earnings Call, May 13, 2026, Girish Wagh: "diesel prices remain a very, very key monitorable... diesel will have at least 30% to 50% impact on the total cost of ownership." |

---

## 6. Margin Drivers By Segment (if applicable)

Not applicable at the reportable segment level. TMCV is a single-segment business for analytical purposes (CV segment = 98.5% of revenue, 103.7% of segment profit). Segment-level margin decomposition within the CV segment (by product line or geography) is not disclosed.

For completeness: the Others segment (insurance broking subsidiary) operates at a higher stated margin (13.9% in FY26) than the consolidated group (12.3% Underlying EBITDA), but at 1.2% of revenue its contribution to consolidated margin movement is negligible.

---

## 7. Margin Bridge — Latest Period

The bridge below covers FY26 (full year) vs FY25 nine-month period. Because the periods are not directly comparable (different durations), the bridge uses percentage-of-revenue movements in basis points to sidestep the period mismatch. The FY24 to FY26 trajectory is used where available from transcript commentary.

| Component | Underlying EBITDA Margin Impact (bps) | Evidence |
|---|---:|---|
| Volume / operating leverage (fixed cost dilution from revenue scaling ~+10% in FY26) | +80 to +120 (estimated) | FQ4 2026 Earnings Call, May 13, 2026: "operating leverage playing out as we scale"; employee cost fell −40 bps as % of revenue; other expenses fell −100 bps. Inference: not precisely decomposed in filing. |
| Pricing and mix (truck ASPs rising; e-bus absence mixed; noncyclical growth positive) | +100 to +150 (estimated) | Q4 FY26: "Higher volume mix, price realization together contributed nearly 250 basis points of EBIT margin improvement" [FQ4 2026 Earnings Call, May 13, 2026]. Annual contribution estimated at roughly half that rate given H1 weakness. |
| Input commodity costs (steel, aluminium, copper — partial pass-through) | −40 to −60 (estimated) | Material cost held at 67.7% vs 67.3% — slight compression of ~40 bps; Q4 alone saw 100 bps variable cost impact from commodities [FQ4 2026 Earnings Call, May 13, 2026, Girish Wagh]. |
| VAVE / cost engineering savings | +30 to +50 (offsetting part of commodity drag) | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), Risk Management, p.40; management cited VAVE covering the un-passed portion of commodity inflation. Inference, not precisely quantified in filing. |
| R&D / product development expensed to P&L (fell as % of revenue) | +50 (from 1.4% to 0.9% of revenue) | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), MD&A, p.214: product development expenses FY26 ₹789 cr (0.9%) vs FY25 ₹814 cr (1.4%). |
| SG&A / other expenses leverage | +100 (from 14.9% to 13.9% of revenue) | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), MD&A, p.216: other expenses ₹11,689 cr (13.9%) vs ₹8,672 cr (14.9%). Scale benefit across warranty, freight, IT, etc. |
| Exceptional items / FVTPL (excluded from Underlying EBITDA) | Neutral (excluded by definition) | FVTPL fair-value loss of ₹2,418 cr excluded from Underlying EBITDA per definition. Exceptional items (₹1,428 cr — stamp duty, new labour code) excluded from Underlying EBITDA. |
| Total Underlying EBITDA margin change (FY25 9M to FY26 full year) | +100 bps (disclosed) | FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), MD&A, p.215: "Underlying EBITDA FY26 = 12.3% vs FY25 = 11.3%" |

**What is missing:** The filing does not publish a formal margin bridge. The component estimates above are derived from cost-line movements in the income statement and management commentary. Each line is labelled as estimate or inference except the total change (which is disclosed). A precise price/volume/cost decomposition is not disclosed.

---

## 8. The Single Biggest Margin Driver

**Commodity input costs — specifically steel, aluminium, and copper.**

At 67.7% of revenue, material costs are by far the largest cost line in TMCV's P&L. The company has no contractual pass-through mechanism with customers. When input costs rise, the company must choose between absorbing the cost (compressing gross margin) or taking price increases that risk damaging demand. In Q4 FY26 alone, elevated commodity prices caused a 100 basis point variable cost impact, and management explicitly declined to pass on the full amount: "we have decided to not pass on the entire commodity increases... we don't want to impact the demand momentum." [FQ4 2026 Earnings Call, May 13, 2026, Girish Wagh] By the time of the same call, management confirmed commodity pressures in Q1 FY27 were "significantly higher" than Q4 FY26. Ashok Leyland (the direct competitor) confirmed the same dynamic on its FQ4 2026 call (May 28, 2026), with steel specifically called out as the predominant source of cost pressure entering Q1 FY27.

The current direction of this driver is a **headwind**. If commodity costs — particularly steel — remain elevated through H1 FY27 and TMCV absorbs even 50–100 bps more than it can pass through in price, gross margin will compress and EBITDA margin will follow directly. The 550 bps structural expansion achieved over FY23–FY26 depended partly on stable-to-falling commodities and rising volumes; both conditions are now under pressure simultaneously as FY27 begins.

---

*Partial-data note: No earnings transcript from Q1–Q3 FY26 individual quarters is in the data pool — only the FQ4 2026 (May 13, 2026) transcript is available. The competitor transcript (Ashok Leyland FQ4 2026, May 28, 2026) is present and used for industry-level commodity context. Quarterly consolidated margin data for Q1 and Q2 FY26 is estimated, not directly disclosed in consolidated form. These limitations are flagged; the margin analysis relies on the audited full-year income statement (MD&A, p.214–219) and the Q4 transcript for forward-looking colour.*

## earnings/04_guidance-consensus.md

# Guidance & Consensus — TMCV

## 1. Consensus Data Metadata

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Source | S&P Global Market Intelligence Estimates (via Transcript Digest cover pages, FQ3 2026 and FQ4 2026) |
| Data as of date | Apr-27-2026 (FQ4 2026 consensus); Jan-27-2026 (FQ3 2026 consensus) |
| Fiscal year basis | India fiscal year ending March 31; S&P labels FY ending Mar 2026 as "FY 2026," FY ending Mar 2027 as "FY 2027" |
| Analyst count | Not stated on cover pages; underlying TataMotorsLimitedNSEITMCVEstimatesReport.xls inaccessible due to OS-level file deadlock — tab structure and analyst count not confirmed |
| Currency | INR (Indian Rupees); revenue in INR millions (mm) on S&P cover; EPS in INR per share |
| Calendarization issue? | Y — TMCV became a standalone listed entity effective October 1, 2025 (demerger from erstwhile Tata Motors Ltd). FY26 covers only the period from June 23, 2024 to March 31, 2026 on a statutory basis, though results are re-presented from July 1, 2024. Pre-demerger comparatives are carved-out CV segment data. Analysts building FY26 consensus were modelling a newly independent entity with limited standalone history; comparability across periods is impaired. |

---

## 2. Management Guidance

TMCV does not provide formal point or range guidance on full-year revenue or EPS. The following guidance was given on the FQ4 2026 earnings call (May 13, 2026) and in the Q4/FY26 results filing (May 13, 2026).

| Metric | Period | Guidance | Type | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Capex | FY27 | 2%–4% of revenue (same range as FY26 actual of ~INR 3,000 crores) | Range | FQ4 2026 transcript, Q&A — G.V. Ramanan: "our guidance would remain similar, 2% to 4% of revenue" |
| EBITDA margin | FY27 | "Teens" (explicitly corrected from "mid-teens" by CFO Ramanan on call) | Qualitative (floor/range implied: ~10–19%; no narrower band stated) | FQ4 2026 transcript, Q&A — Ramanan: "our guidance was teens and not mid-teens" |
| Volume growth (India CV TIV) | Q1 FY27 | Single-digit growth "if not more"; management noted uncertainty from diesel price headwinds and Operation Sindoor-related sentiment suppression in H1 | Qualitative / directional | FQ4 2026 transcript, Q&A — Girish Wagh: "we should see a single-digit growth, if not more, in Q1" |
| International volume | FY27 | Quarter-by-quarter approach cited due to Middle East / MENA uncertainty; Indonesia 70,000-unit order deliveries ramping | Qualitative — no number given | FQ4 2026 transcript, Q&A — Girish Wagh |
| Iveco acquisition closure | Q2 FY27 | Transaction closure targeted by Q2 FY27; financing via bridge loan initially; equity/debt split to be finalised closer to date | Point (timing) | FQ4 2026 transcript, prepared remarks — G.V. Ramanan; also May 13, 2026 filing Note 7 (total consideration ~INR 41,691 crores / €3.8 billion) |
| Dividend | FY26 (declared) | INR 4 per share final dividend recommended (subject to shareholder approval); cash outflow ~INR 1,500 crores | Point | FQ4 2026 transcript, prepared remarks — G.V. Ramanan; May 13, 2026 filing Note 13 |
| Revenue | FY27 | No formal guidance given | N/A | FQ4 2026 transcript — no revenue target stated |
| EPS | FY27 | No formal guidance given | N/A | FQ4 2026 transcript — no EPS target stated |

**Key qualitative commentary on FY27 outlook (FQ4 2026 transcript, May 13, 2026):**

- Commodity headwinds described as "certainly serious" — around 100 basis points impact in Q4 FY26 already, with "significantly higher impact being seen in Q1" FY27. Management took a 2% price increase in April 2026 but decided not to pass on the full commodity cost increase, absorbing a portion on the cost side.
- Demand fundamentals described as "robust" despite near-term headwinds. April 2026 showed "healthy double-digit growth" in industry volumes.
- H1 FY27 expected to be softer ("H1 was muted" language echoing FY26 experience) weighted by Operation Sindoor sentiment and early monsoon onset. H2 recovery expected, consistent with FY26's back-weighted pattern.
- Parts and services business — goal to grow portfolio and digitise supply chain; noncyclical revenue CAGR running at 2.7x cyclical CAGR per FY26 presentation.
- Iveco: financing not yet finalised; bridge loan plus possible equity; deal not reflected in FY27 guidance because closure is mid-year and structure is undetermined.

---

## 3. Guidance vs Consensus Table

**Notes on construction:**
- Revenue: consensus in INR millions (mm) per S&P; filing revenue in INR crores (1 crore = 10 mm, so INR 83,855 crores = INR 838,550mm). FY26 consensus from S&P FQ4 2026 cover = INR 813,308.51mm. Actuals from filing = INR 838,550mm (consolidated, FY26).
- EPS: S&P uses "Normalized EPS." FY26 S&P consensus = INR 17.82; actual normalized = INR 15.91 (per S&P FQ4 2026 cover). The Ind AS filing reports basic/diluted EPS of INR 8.23 on a fully consolidated basis — the difference versus S&P normalized reflects S&P's normalization adjustments (adding back exceptional items and FVTPL losses). The two EPS series are not directly comparable; normalized EPS is used throughout this table to stay on a consistent S&P basis.
- Management provides no point or range guidance for FY27 revenue or EPS, so Section 3 compares actuals versus prior consensus for FY26, and shows FY27 consensus as a forward read.
- Gap = Consensus minus Guidance. Where no formal guidance exists, the FY27 column shows consensus only with "No guidance — gap not calculable."

**FY26 actuals vs prior consensus (bar that was set heading into the year):**

| Metric | Period | Consensus (heading in) | Actual | Gap (Actual − Consensus) | Direction |
|---|---|---|---|---:|---|
| Revenue (INR mm) | FY26 | 813,308.51 (as of Apr-27-2026 per S&P FQ4 cover) | 838,550.00 | +25,241.49 (+3.1%) | Actual beat consensus |
| EPS Normalized (INR) | FY26 | 17.82 (as of Apr-27-2026 per S&P FQ4 cover) | 15.91 | −1.91 (−10.7%) | Actual missed consensus |

*Interpretation: Revenue beat consensus for FY26 by ~3%, but normalized EPS missed consensus by ~11%. This gap — revenue above but EPS below — is consistent with the commodity cost absorption management described on the call (higher volumes, but material cost inflation partly absorbed rather than passed through).*

**FY27 consensus (forward bar, no management guidance to compare):**

| Metric | Period | Management Guidance | Street Consensus (as of Apr-27-2026) | Gap | Gap Direction |
|---|---|---|---|---:|---|
| Revenue (INR mm) | FY27 | No formal guidance | 881,357.24 | Not calculable | N/A |
| EPS Normalized (INR) | FY27 | No formal guidance | 19.17 | Not calculable | N/A |
| EBITDA margin | FY27 | "Teens" (qualitative; corrected from "mid-teens") | Not separately visible from available cover pages | Qualitative only — no number | N/A |
| Capex | FY27 | 2%–4% of revenue (~INR 1,763–3,525 crores at FY27 consensus revenue) | Not separately visible | Range only | N/A |

**Implicit revenue growth embedded in FY27 consensus:** INR 881,357mm vs FY26 actual INR 838,550mm = +5.1% growth. This is a modest growth assumption, consistent with management's qualitative "single-digit growth" language for Q1 and the uncertain FY27 backdrop.

**Implicit EPS growth embedded in FY27 consensus:** INR 19.17 vs FY26 actual normalized INR 15.91 = +20.5% growth. This is meaningfully above the revenue growth rate, implying analysts expect margin expansion or lower exceptional costs in FY27. Given FY26 contained INR 1,428 crores of net exceptional losses (new labour code, merger costs, Iveco acquisition costs), a partial normalisation in FY27 would mechanically boost reported EPS even with flat EBITDA margins. Whether "teens" EBITDA guidance (which the CFO refused to narrow to mid-teens) supports this level of EPS growth is not certain — the Street appears to be embedding significant exceptional-item recovery.

---

## 4. Estimate Revision Momentum Table

The underlying TataMotorsLimitedNSEITMCVEstimatesReport.xls is inaccessible due to an OS-level file deadlock (OSError: Resource deadlock avoided), so the revision history tab cannot be read directly. Two data points are available from the S&P transcript cover pages, which show consensus at two separate moments.

| Estimate | As of Jan-27-2026 (FQ3 cover) | As of Apr-27-2026 (FQ4 cover) | Change | Direction |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| Revenue FY26 (INR mm) | 806,586.77 | 813,308.51 | +6,721.74 (+0.8%) | Rising (modest) |
| EPS Normalized FY26 (INR) | 16.25 | 17.82 | +1.57 (+9.7%) | Rising |
| Revenue FY27 (INR mm) | 884,200.07 | 881,357.24 | −2,842.83 (−0.3%) | Marginally falling |
| EPS Normalized FY27 (INR) | 19.22 | 19.17 | −0.05 (−0.3%) | Essentially flat |
| Revenue FQ4 2026 (INR mm) | 212,717.00 | 237,013.00 | +24,296.00 (+11.4%) | Rising sharply |
| EPS Normalized FQ4 2026 (INR) | 3.73 | 5.20 | +1.47 (+39.4%) | Rising sharply |

*Two-snapshot limitation: these are only two points in time separated by ~three months. True revision momentum (90/60/30-day trend) cannot be constructed — only the delta between the Jan-27-2026 and Apr-27-2026 snapshots is available. A full revision series from the estimates workbook would be needed to confirm the direction.*

**Observations from available snapshots:**
- FY26 estimates moved up between January and April, with EPS revised up +9.7% after the strong Q3 beat (+18.57% EPS surprise).
- FQ4 estimates rose sharply between snapshots (+39.4% EPS, +11.4% revenue), likely lifted by the Q3 beat and strong volume data.
- FY27 estimates were essentially unchanged across the two snapshots — suggesting analysts did not meaningfully change their FY27 model after the Q3 results, possibly awaiting more clarity on Iveco and commodity costs.
- After the FQ4 2026 results (May 13, 2026), the Street will need to revise FY27 estimates to account for: (a) Q4 EPS beat of +10.7%; (b) commodity headwind warnings; (c) H1 FY27 softness guidance; (d) Iveco financing drag. The direction of post-Q4 revisions is not yet visible in the available data (consensus is as of Apr-27-2026, before the Q4 results).

---

## 5. Revision Breadth

*Revision breadth not available — only aggregate estimate levels from two snapshots. No analyst-level up/down revision count is accessible from the available data (estimates workbook inaccessible; cover pages show only aggregate consensus).*

---

## 6. Historical Beat / Miss Pattern

Based on the two transcript digest cover pages available in the pool:

| Period | Revenue Beat/Miss | EPS Beat/Miss | Magnitude | Notes |
|---|---|---|---:|---|
| FQ3 2026 (Oct–Dec 2025) | Beat | Beat | Revenue +20.70%; EPS +18.57% | Consensus as of Jan-27-2026; first full standalone quarter post-demerger; consensus set low given entity had no listed track record |
| FQ4 2026 (Jan–Mar 2026) | Beat | Beat | Revenue +3.64%; EPS +10.70% | Consensus as of Apr-27-2026; Q4 had typical seasonal strength; still above consensus despite elevated commodity costs in quarter |
| FQ2 2026 (Jul–Sep 2025) | N/A | N/A | N/A | TMCV listed in November 2025; no S&P consensus cover available for Q2 in the data pool |
| FQ1 2026 (Apr–Jun 2025) | N/A | N/A | N/A | Pre-listing; no consensus data |

*Two-quarter pattern only; the entity's listed history is short. Both available quarters show beats, with FQ3 beating by a much wider margin (likely because consensus was newly formed for a just-listed company with limited visibility). FQ4 beat was narrower but still positive on both revenue and EPS despite commodity cost headwinds.*

**FY26 full year (annual check):** Revenue beat consensus (+3.1%) but normalized EPS missed consensus by −10.7%. The quarterly beats did not add up to a full-year EPS beat, because the FY26 full-year consensus (INR 17.82) was set above what the company delivered (INR 15.91 normalized). This suggests analysts had modelled faster EPS growth for the second half of the year than materialised — likely due to the elevated exceptional items (INR 1,428 crores net exceptional loss in FY26) and commodity cost absorption.

---

## 7. Bar Assessment

**Bar is low.**

The FY27 Street consensus (EPS INR 19.17, revenue INR 881,357mm as of Apr-27-2026) was set before the Q4 FY26 results landed. Q4 delivered a +10.7% EPS beat and a +3.6% revenue beat. The Q4 beat will mechanically require upward FY27 revisions from at least some analysts once the post-results cycle completes — the consensus available here is pre-Q4 actuals. More importantly, management's qualitative FY27 guidance — "teens" EBITDA (not mid-teens), "single-digit growth if not more" for Q1, and a cautious stance on commodity and MENA headwinds — is deliberately conservative and leaves room to outperform the embedded +20.5% EPS growth assumption in FY27 consensus. The two-quarter beat history (+18.6% and +10.7% EPS surprises) was driven partly by low initial consensus on a newly listed stock with no track record, and the FQ3 bar effect will moderate over time. However, the combination of: (a) consensus set before Q4 results confirmed continued execution, (b) a 2% April price increase partially offsetting commodity costs, (c) Indonesia 70,000-unit order now physically in delivery, and (d) management's refusal to guide to mid-teens margin (implying potential upside to actual margin delivery) all point to a bar that is currently set below what the business is likely to deliver in FY27, assuming commodity and MENA headwinds do not intensify materially beyond the current level.

The primary risk to this assessment: commodity cost absorption is already eating margin in Q1 FY27, the Iveco financing structure is unresolved (bridge loan could be dilutive), and H1 FY27 is expected to be softer than H2 — which means near-term results may disappoint even if the full-year bar proves beatable.


---
## earnings/05_beat-miss-setup.md

# Beat / Miss Setup — TMCV

**Reporting regime:** India / SEBI-LODR. Reporting standard: Ind AS. Currency: INR (₹ crores). Fiscal year ends March 31. All consensus data sourced from S&P Global Market Intelligence Estimates as of April 27, 2026 (pre-Q4 FY26 results release). Consensus data exists; partial-data rule does not apply.

---

## 1. Next Quarter Context

The next reporting quarter is Q1 FY27 (April 1 to June 30, 2026), results expected in late July or early August 2026. This is the seasonally weakest quarter for Indian commercial vehicle (CV) manufacturers: monsoon onset suppresses construction and freight activity in June, fleet owner purchasing decisions are deferred, and the annual base effect from a strong Q4 FY26 (₹26,098 cr revenue, 13.1% EBITDA margin — the best quarter of the year) creates a tough sequential comparison. The Street's FY27 consensus (revenue ₹881,357mm / ~₹88,136 cr; normalized EPS ₹19.17) was set before the Q4 FY26 results landed and embeds +5.1% revenue growth and +20.5% EPS growth over the FY26 actuals. Q1 will almost certainly print below Q4's absolute levels in both revenue and margin, so the question is not sequential improvement — it is whether the company can deliver enough in Q1 to keep the full-year bar intact, while navigating commodity cost inflation that management described as "significantly higher" than the 100 basis-point hit already absorbed in Q4 FY26.

---

## 2. Beat Scenarios

| Scenario | Driver | What Would Need To Happen | Likelihood | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Volume tracks mid-to-high single-digit better than "flat to down" fear | Domestic CV industry demand (02: freight activity, e-way bills, transporter profitability) | India industry TIV grows mid-to-high single-digit in Q1 FY27 as April already showed MHCV retail +20% YoY; TMCV holds or gains share at 39–40%; Indonesia 70,000-unit order begins contributing meaningfully to export line | High | April 2026 MHCV retail +20% YoY [Web: Business Standard, May 2026, unverified]; management guided "single-digit if not more" [FQ4 2026 Earnings Call, May 13, 2026, Girish Wagh]; Indonesia 70,000-unit order in delivery phase [FQ4 2026 Earnings Call, May 13, 2026, p.5] |
| Commodity pass-through better than feared; gross margin holds | Commodity input costs / VAVE savings (03: single biggest margin driver) | Steel prices stabilise or ease from Q4 FY26 levels; VAVE programme offsets a larger portion of Q1 impact than the market fears; 2% April price increase (taken ahead of Q1) absorbs 50–70% of commodity cost increase rather than the 40–50% management implied | Mid | Steel HR coil at ₹54,800–60,450/tonne as of June 2026, firm but not spiking [Web: BankBazaar, June 3, 2026, unverified]; VAVE described as "institutionalised and continuous" [FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), Risk Management, p.40]; April 2% price increase confirmed [FQ4 2026 Earnings Call, May 13, 2026, Q&A] |
| Non-cyclical revenue mix lift (parts, Fleet Edge, aftermarket) | Non-cyclical downstream revenue (02: services, 03: margin mix) | Parts and services continue their 18.2% FY26 growth pace independently of Q1 volume seasonality; Fleet Edge subscription renewals (which doubled Q1–Q4 in FY26) contribute high-margin recurring revenue that is invisible to a volume-focused consensus model | Mid | Non-cyclical CAGR 2.7x cyclical [FQ4 2026 Earnings Call, May 13, 2026, G.V. Ramanan, p.4]; Fleet Edge >1 million connected vehicles, subscription renewals doubling [FQ4 2026 Earnings Call, May 13, 2026, p.6]; parts and services +18.2% in FY26 [FY26 Integrated Annual Report (Ind AS), Chairman's Letter, p.9] |
| Consensus revision lift: analysts upgrade FY27 estimates post-Q4 beat | Historical beat / miss pattern + guidance-consensus gap (04: bar assessment) | Post-Q4 estimate revisions (Q4 EPS +10.7% beat, revenue +3.6% beat) cause analysts to mark up Q1 FY27 and FY27 EPS estimates; TMCV outperforms the newly revised bar even if the absolute Q1 print looks modest in isolation | Low–Mid | Q4 FY26 EPS beat +10.7% [FQ4 2026 Earnings Call Transcript cover, May 13, 2026]; FY27 consensus as of Apr-27-2026 pre-Q4 actuals [04_guidance-consensus.md, S&P GMI]; analysts had not yet incorporated Q4 beat into FY27 at the available snapshot date |

---

## 3. Miss Scenarios

| Scenario | Driver | What Would Need To Happen | Likelihood | Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Commodity cost absorption is larger than the 2% April price increase covers; gross margin compresses 150–200 bps | Commodity input costs — steel, aluminium, copper (03: single biggest margin driver) | Steel and other input costs remain elevated or rise further in Q1 FY27; TMCV absorbs 50–100 bps beyond what pricing covers (as it did in Q4 — took only partial pass-through to protect demand); Q1 EBITDA margin falls to 11.0–11.5% vs FY26's 12.3% level, missing consensus that embeds margin expansion | High | Management: "significantly higher impact being seen in Q1" vs Q4's 100 bps [FQ4 2026 Earnings Call, May 13, 2026, Girish Wagh]; steel prices firm in June 2026 [Web: BankBazaar, June 3, 2026, unverified]; management decided not to pass full commodity increase [FQ4 2026 Earnings Call, Q&A]; Ashok Leyland CFO confirmed steel as sector-wide Q1 pressure [FQ4 2026 Earnings Call, May 13, 2026] |
| H1 FY27 volume is flat-to-down vs Q4 FY26; operating leverage reverses | Domestic CV demand — monsoon seasonality + diesel price risk (02: end-market demand, fleet operator profitability) | Monsoon suppresses June freight activity; diesel price remains elevated, compressing transporter margins and deferring HCV purchase decisions; Q1 volume comes in below 110,000 units (vs Q4's 131,800 units), driving a sharp sequential drop in EBITDA as fixed costs deleverage | Mid–High | Q4 FY26 wholesale 131,800 units, Q1 FY26 ~55,000–65,000 (estimated, derived; the H2 weighting of ~57% in FY26 implies H1 was ~43%) [01_historical-financials.md, derived estimate]; diesel "very, very key monitorable" [FQ4 2026 Earnings Call, May 13, 2026, p.7]; H1 FY27 expected "muted" per management tone [04_guidance-consensus.md] |
| Iveco acquisition closes in Q2 FY27 as guided, generating large exceptional charges and bridge loan interest that hits Q1–Q2 P&L | Iveco acquisition financing — exceptional items and interest costs (03: Iveco integration costs) | €3.875 billion bridge loan at Euribor +102.5 bps begins accruing once deal closes; even if Q1 FY27 is pre-close, deal costs (legal, advisory, regulatory) and potential write-offs appear as exceptionals; full-year EPS impact from ~₹3,000–4,000 crore annual interest on the bridge loan causes the Street's EPS estimates to look optimistic | Mid | Bridge loan €3.875 billion at Euribor +102.5bps, 12-month term [Web: Yahoo Finance / Automotive World, unverified]; Tata Motors to raise €1 billion equity via rights issue or QIP and monetise Tata Capital stake [Web: Autocar Professional, unverified]; deal closure targeted Q2 FY27 [FQ4 2026 Earnings Call, May 13, 2026, G.V. Ramanan]; Street's FY27 EPS consensus (+20.5% growth) does not appear to reflect the full interest drag [04_guidance-consensus.md] |
